name: Botai Culture
desc: Late Neolithic/Eneolithic culture of northern Kazakhstan (3700–3100 BCE), renowned for pioneering the earliest known horse domestication based on dental bit wear, pottery lipid residues and mass horse culling patterns. Specialised horse-focused economy with semi-sedentary large pit-house settlements of up to 150+ houses; incised geometric pottery, microlithic industry. Ancient genomes confirm indigenous north-central steppe ancestry with long-term local continuity, genetically distinct from both Eastern hunter-gatherers and later Yamnaya steppe pastoralists.

sites.name: Botai
sites.desc: Type site (Akmola region, N Kazakhstan); the largest Botai settlement with over 150 pit-houses, mass horse bone deposits (up to 99% of faunal remains), earliest bit wear evidence on equid teeth at 3500–3300 BCE

sites.name: Krasny Yar
sites.desc: Major fortified Botai settlement on upper Ishim River; multiple pit-houses, horse corral structures, copper-working evidence

sites.name: Vasilkovka
sites.desc: Key Botai settlement cluster with dense pit-house occupation; abundant pottery and lithic assemblage

sites.name: Balandino
sites.desc: Northern Botai site on Tobol River; transitional local variant with mixed horse-fishing subsistence economy

sites.name: Roschinskoe
sites.desc: Eastern Botai settlement; faunal remains documenting horse herd demography and seasonal slaughter patterns

papers.title: Damgaard et al. (2018) The first horse herders and the impact of early Bronze Age steppe expansions into Asia
Outram et al. (2009) The earliest horse harnessing and milking
Bendrey (2012) The Botai culture and horse domestication

name: Yamnaya Culture
desc: Late Eneolithic to Early Bronze Age pastoral culture of the Pontic-Caspian Steppe (3300–2600 BCE). Famous for kurgan burials, mobile pastoralism, and being a major genetic source for Indo-European expansions across Europe and Asia.

sites.name: Mykhailivka
sites.desc: Type-site settlement on lower Dnieper River (Zaporizhzhia, Ukraine); Early Yamnaya formative layer 3600–3300 BCE, benchmark for Western Pontic Yamnaya material typology, rare permanent residential site among nomadic Yamnaya communities.

sites.name: Repin
sites.desc: Repin Culture core site along Middle Don River; Late Eneolithic transitional precursor complex directly evolving into Don variant Early Yamnaya, key for chronological division between pre-Yamnaya and classic Yamnaya.

sites.name: Khvalynsk
sites.desc: Large Middle Volga Eneolithic cemetery; Khvalynsk culture is primary Volga-Ural ancestral substratum for Eastern Yamnaya, predates classic Yamnaya by 500+ years (c.4500–3800 BCE).

sites.name: Novosvobodnaya
sites.desc: North Caucasus piedmont transitional kurgan site; Late Yamnaya frontier settlement revealing intensive genetic and material exchange between steppe Yamnaya and local North Caucasus farming populations.

sites.name: Samara
sites.desc: Samara River valley clustered kurgan necropolises (Middle Volga, Russia); primary ancient DNA sampling hotspot for Eastern Yamnaya, source of canonical Yamnaya autosomal reference genomes in global archaeogenetics.

sites.name: Boldyrevo-IV
sites.desc: Orenburg (South Urals) elite kurgan cluster; hosts the largest single-diameter Yamnaya burial mound in Volga-Ural region, representative Eastern Yamnaya high-status interment custom.

papers.title: Haak et al. (2015) Massive migration from the steppe was a source for Indo-European languages in Europe
Anthony (2007) The Horse, the Wheel, and Language
Damgaard et al. (2018) The first horse herders and the impact of early Bronze Age steppe expansions into Asia

name: Afonasiev Culture
desc: Early Bronze Age pastoral and metallurgical culture in southern Siberia, Altai, western Mongolia and northern Xinjiang (3300–2500 BCE); regarded as the easternmost extension of the Yamnaya horizon, with strong genetic links to early Indo-European speakers.

sites.name: Afonasiev Gora
sites.desc: Type site near Bateni-Yarki village, Minusinsk Basin; eponymous cemetery excavated 1920s, now submerged by Krasnoyarsk Reservoir

sites.name: Kara-Koba
sites.desc: Key ritual site in central Gorny Altai; circular stone enclosure with stelae, typical Afonasiev ceremonial complex

sites.name: Kucherla 1
sites.desc: Sacred stone-circle site in Altai Mountains; central pit with pottery fragments, used for communal rituals

sites.name: Ust-Kuyum
sites.desc: Well-preserved burial site in Altai; early Afonasiev horizon (3300–3000 BCE) with classic kurgan burials

sites.name: Aitokhan 1
sites.desc: Northern Xinjiang (China) cemetery; two Afonasiev-style stone-cist kurgans (M21, M22), easternmost confirmed site

sites.name: Altan Sandal
sites.desc: Central Mongolia burial site; eastern expansion marker, Afonasiev radiocarbon dates 3000–2800 BCE

sites.name: Shatar Chuluu
sites.desc: Mongolian steppe cemetery; easternmost Afonasiev complex, demonstrates 1,500 km eastward spread from Altai

sites.name: Ukok Plateau Burials
sites.desc: High-altitude Altai burial ground; frozen tombs with well-preserved organic remains, key for DNA studies

sites.name: Vladimirovka
sites.desc: Copper-mining settlement in Minusinsk Basin; evidence of early metallurgy, rare Afonasiev industrial site

sites.name: Askat 2
sites.desc: Altai settlement with bronze-casting mould fragments; confirms early metal production in Afonasiev communities

papers.title: Allentoft et al. (2015) Population genomics of Bronze Age Eurasia
Narasimhan et al. (2019) The formation of human populations in South and Central Asia
Damgaard et al. (2018) The first horse herders and the impact of early Bronze Age steppe expansions into Asia

name: Sintashta Culture
desc: Middle Bronze Age culture of the Southern Urals and northern Kazakhstan (2200–1900 BCE). Famous for fortified circular settlements, the earliest known spoked-wheel chariots, large-scale metallurgy and elite kurgan burials; closely linked to early Indo-Iranian peoples.

sites.name: Sintashta
sites.desc: Type site, fortified settlement and necropolis; yielded the world's earliest spoked-wheel chariot burials

sites.name: Arkaim
sites.desc: Iconic double-ring circular fortified settlement, major ceremonial and residential centre

sites.name: Kamenny Ambar
sites.desc: Large fortified site with intensive bronze metallurgy and multiple elite burials

sites.name: Petrovka
sites.desc: Key site of the Sintashta-Petrovka complex, important for regional chronology

sites.name: Ustye
sites.desc: Multi-layer settlement with Sintashta occupation over earlier Poltavka cultural remains

sites.name: Tobolskoye
sites.desc: Easternmost major Sintashta settlement on the upper Tobol River

papers.title: Allentoft et al. (2015) Population genomics of Bronze Age Eurasia
Narasimhan et al. (2019) The formation of human populations in South and Central Asia

name: Seima-Turbino Phenomenon
desc: Transcultural bronze-working network across northern Eurasia (2200–1900 BCE), famous for advanced metalwork and wide geographic spread.

sites.name: Seima
sites.desc: Type cemetery near Nizhny Novgorod, Volga‑Oka confluence; rich bronze weapon hoard, type site of the phenomenon

sites.name: Turbino
sites.desc: Type cemetery near Perm, Urals; bronze spearheads, combat knives, and early ST burial rites

sites.name: Kaninskaya Cave
sites.desc: Northernmost ST site in Pechora Urals; ritual cave with bronze offerings, northern boundary marker

sites.name: Rostovka
sites.desc: Key burial site near Omsk; high‑quality ancient DNA, bronze weapons, and Siberian ancestry evidence

sites.name: Satyga 16
sites.desc: Trans‑Urals cemetery; bronze implements, human remains, and early ST metallurgical traces

sites.name: Samus‑4
sites.desc: Settlement & workshop in Tomsk‑Narym; bronze‑casting site, core ST metallurgical production center

sites.name: Tatarka
sites.desc: Easternmost major ST site on Yenisei; eastern boundary marker, Siberian genetic affinities

sites.name: Reshnoe
sites.desc: Important ST cemetery in Western Siberia; bronze daggers, axes, and typical burial assemblages

sites.name: Ust’‑Vetluga
sites.desc: Volga‑Oka region cemetery; early ST bronze tools, transitional cultural layers

sites.name: Elunino 1
sites.desc: Ob‑Irtysh steppe cemetery; Elunin culture overlap, ST bronze ornaments and weapons

papers.title: Childebayeva et al. (2024) Bronze age Northern Eurasian genetics in the context of development of metallurgy and Siberian ancestry
papers.org: Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology

name: Andronovo Culture
desc: Bronze Age pastoral and metallurgical culture (2000–1450 BCE) distributed across Southern Urals, Western Siberia and Central Asia. Closely associated with early Indo-Iranian groups, featuring kurgan burials, mobile herding and advanced bronze production.

sites.name: Andronovo
sites.desc: Type cemetery near Andronovo village, Bashkortostan; the eponymous site defining the culture

sites.name: Petrovka
sites.desc: Key site of the early Petrovka phase, core area of Northern Kazakhstan

sites.name: Alakul
sites.desc: Representative site of the Alakul regional variant, widely spread across northern Kazakhstan

sites.name: Fedorovo
sites.desc: Classic site of the Fedorovo phase, dominant in Southern Siberia and Southern Urals

sites.name: Alekseyevka
sites.desc: Large kurgan burial complex in eastern Kazakhstan with typical Andronovo burial assemblages

sites.name: Sabinka 2
sites.desc: Well-preserved burial ground in Altai Krai, widely used for ancient genome research

sites.name: Arban 1
sites.desc: Cemetery in Upper Ob region, reflects Andronovo influence in Western Siberia

sites.name: Adunqiaolu
sites.desc: Easternmost major Andronovo site in northern Xinjiang, marks the eastern expansion boundary

papers.title: Narasimhan et al. (2019) The formation of human populations in South and Central Asia
Allentoft et al. (2015) Population genomics of Bronze Age Eurasia

name: Chemurchek Culture
desc: Early Bronze Age culture of Western Mongolia and Altai (2750–1900 BCE), known for rectangular stone enclosures, stelae, and mixed pastoral farming; post-Afanasievo cultural complex.

sites.name: Chemurchek
sites.desc: Type site in Altai China; rectangular stone burial enclosures and ceremonial stelae

sites.name: Yagshiin Khödöö
sites.desc: Key Chemurchek burial complex in Khovd, Western Mongolia

sites.name: Kulala-Ula
sites.desc: Well-preserved stone enclosure site in Bayan-Ölgii, Mongolia

sites.name: Kurgak-Govi
sites.desc: Mongolian Altai burial site with Chemurchek and Afanasievo overlap

sites.name: Dunde Bulaq
sites.desc: Ritual and habitation site in northern Xinjiang with Chemurchek remains

papers.title: Fan Zhang (2023) The genomic origins of the Bronze Age Tarim Basin mummies
Damgaard et al. (2018) The first horse herders and impact of early Bronze Age steppe expansions into Asia
Narasimhan et al. (2019) The formation of human populations in South and Central Asia

name: Okunev Culture
desc: Middle Bronze Age culture of Minusinsk Basin, Tuva and Northern Mongolia (2600–1750 BCE), famous for stone stelae, kurgan burials and local Siberian pastoral traditions.

sites.name: Okunev Ulus
sites.desc: Type site of Okunev culture; classic stone burial cairns and anthropomorphic stelae

sites.name: Uibat V
sites.desc: Major Okunev necropolis in Minusinsk Basin, Russia

sites.name: Chebaki Sve
sites.desc: Mountain sanctuary and fortified ceremonial center of Okunev culture

sites.name: Zhelvak 5
sites.desc: Okunev habitation and burial site in Tuva Republic

sites.name: Lake Khövsgöl
sites.desc: Northern Mongolia outpost of Okunev burial and pastoral activity

papers.title: Allentoft et al. (2015) Population genomics of Bronze Age Eurasia
Narasimhan et al. (2019) The formation of human populations in South and Central Asia
Damgaard et al. (2018) The first horse herders and the impact of early Bronze Age steppe expansions into Asia

name: Ulaanzuukh‑Tevsh Culture
desc: Late Early to Middle Bronze Age culture of eastern Mongolia (c.1450–1000 BCE). Ancient Northeast Asian (ANA) pastoralists with slab‑built burial monuments, antecedent to Slab Grave culture.

sites.name: Ulaanzuukh
sites.desc: Type site; large rectangular slab enclosures, ritual monuments

sites.name: Tevsh
sites.desc: Key eastern variant site; circular and rectangular burial complexes

sites.name: Dornod Survey Cluster
sites.desc: Dense Ulaanzuukh monumental cemetery area

sites.name: Delgerkhan Uul
sites.desc: Ridge with concentrations of Ulaanzuukh stone monuments

sites.name: Sümber Mountain
sites.desc: Southern Gobi Ulaanzuukh ritual and burial site

papers.title: Wright et al. (2019) The earliest Bronze Age culture of the south‑eastern Gobi Desert
Jeong et al. (2020) A dynamic 6,000‑year genetic history of Eurasia’s eastern steppe

name: Glazkovo Culture
desc: Bronze Age hunter‑gatherer/fisher culture around Lake Baikal (c.2200–1300 BCE). Known for pottery, copper artifacts, and burials with stone cairns; likely proto‑Tungusic.

sites.name: Glazkov (Type site)
sites.desc: Suburb of Irkutsk; first discovered Glazkovo burial ground

sites.name: Olkhon Island
sites.desc: Sacred island with multiple Glazkovo cemeteries

sites.name: Kurma XI
sites.desc: Early Bronze Age cemetery with Glazkovo burials

sites.name: Selenga Delta
sites.desc: Southern Baikal large settlement & burial cluster

papers.title: Weber et al. (2002) Prehistoric Foragers of the Cis‑Baikal
Damgaard et al. (2018) 137 ancient genomes across Eurasia

name: Serovo Culture
desc: Late Neolithic hunter‑gatherer culture (c.3300–2200 BCE) preceding Glazkovo; distinctive pottery, bone tools, and extended burials.

sites.name: Serovo (Type site)
sites.desc: Former village; classic Serovo cemetery under Bratsk reservoir

sites.name: Little Sea Microregion
sites.desc: Intensively studied Serovo burial cluster on Lake Baikal

papers.title: Goriunova et al. (2020) Middle Holocene hunter‑gatherer mortuary practices

name: Deer Stone-Khirigsuur Complex (DSKC)
desc: Late Bronze Age pastoralist archaeological complex distributed across northern Mongolia, southern Tuva and western Sayan foothills, evolving from the late Khövsgöl Late Bronze Age formative culture. Fully mobile specialized ruminant pastoral economy centered on cattle, sheep, goat and early horse husbandry with confirmed ancient dairy consumption; defined by two iconic ritual monuments: carved anthropomorphic deer stone stelae and enclosed khirigsuur stone mortuary complexes consisting of central burial cairns plus peripheral sacrificial animal pits. Material culture dominated by arsenic bronze daggers, socketed arrowheads, rare coarse handmade pottery and stone personal ornaments; critical ancestral population source for subsequent Slab Grave Culture and early Xiongnu pastoral groups of the Eastern Eurasian steppe.

sites.name: Khavtsal II
sites.desc: Type core cemetery near Lake Khövsgöl, northern Mongolia; late Khövsgöl LBA evolving into early DSKC layers, dozens of paired deer stone + khirigsuur burials, key radiocarbon dated benchmark 1180–890 BCE.

sites.name: Arbulag
sites.desc: Large lakeshore DSKC necropolis cluster on southern Khövsgöl lakeside; primary site for dental calculus ancient dairy residue testing confirming steppe milk pastoralism.

sites.name: Ulaan Taiga
sites.desc: Northern frontier DSKC site on Mongolia-Tuva border; high concentration of classic mature-style carved deer stones, northernmost core distribution marker of DSKC.

sites.name: Bugat
sites.desc: Central Mongolia transitional DSKC cemetery; southern marginal site showing material exchange with contemporary Ulaanzuukh Culture communities.

sites.name: Kyzyl-Dag
sites.desc: Tuva (Russia) western DSKC outpost site; khirigsuur monuments alongside Altai Bronze Age artefacts recording westward cultural communication with Chemurchek groups.

sites.name: Terkhiin Tsagaan Nuur
sites.desc: Western Mongolia lake basin DSKC site group; scattered isolated deer stones and small khirigsuur mounds marking southwest boundary of the complex.

papers.title: Deer Stone-Khirigsuur Culture of Northern Mongolia (2017), Mongolian Institute of Archaeology, foundational regional monograph
Wilkin et al. (2018) Ancient dental calculus reveals early dairy pastoralism in Khövsgöl DSKC populations, PNAS
papers.org: Max Planck Institute for the Science of Human History

papers.title: Damgaard et al. (2018) Genome-wide data from Bronze Age Mongolia links DSKC ancestry to later Xiongnu populations, Nature Communications

name: Yumin Culture
desc: Early Neolithic culture on the northern piedmont of eastern Yin Mountains and southeastern Inner Mongolian Plateau, dated 6600–5000 BCE (8600–7000 BP). The earliest confirmed Neolithic archaeological culture in Inner Mongolia. Economy dominated by hunting-foraging supplemented with primitive millet dry-farming; semi-sedentary seasonal camp settlements of semi-subterranean round/oval pit houses. Defined by sophisticated microlithic industry, unique pointed/round-bottom & flake-shaped low-fired pottery with pit-impressed decoration, abundant bone tools; lays foundational precedent for later mixed farming-pastoral traditions of North China steppe-ecotone. Named after the type-site Yumin, Huade County, Ulanqab, excavated from 2010 and formally defined as independent culture in 2015.

sites.name: Yumin
sites.desc: Type site in Huade County (Ulanqab, Inner Mongolia), core defining site of Yumin Culture; semi-subterranean pit-house seasonal settlement, diagnostic pointed-bottom pottery and full microlithic assemblage, dated 6400–6000 BCE (8400–8000 BP), excavated 2010–2016

sites.name: Simagou
sites.desc: Key Yumin settlement in Huade County; dual seasonal occupation layers, carbonized millet recovered confirming early dry farming, 6100–5500 BCE

sites.name: Narren Tolgoi
sites.desc: Large cluster site in Xianghuang Banner, Xilingol; over 200 semi-subterranean houses, rich faunal & charcoal remains, dated 5800–5400 BCE

sites.name: Xinglong
sites.desc: Typical border site in Kangbao County, Zhangjiakou (Hebei); early Yumin cultural layers, mixed hunter-cultivator material assemblage

sites.name: Sitai
sites.desc: Core Hebei-side site in Shangyi County; Phase IV deposits belong to late Yumin Culture, critical for cross-border cultural research between Inner Mongolia and North Hebei

sites.name: Daihai
sites.desc: Liangcheng Daihai lake-basin site cluster; marginal late-phase Yumin occupation layers overlain by later mid-Neolithic cultural deposits

sites.name: Baimiaozi
sites.desc: Chahar Right Middle Banner settlement & cemetery; standardized Yumin pit-house architecture and distinctive squat-burial customs without grave goods

papers.title: Preliminary Research on Yumin Culture (2015), Inner Mongolia Institute of Cultural Relics and Archaeology (official foundational monograph)
papers.org: Inner Mongolia Institute of Cultural Relics and Archaeology

papers.title: Sun et al. (2021) Ancient genomes document early millet agriculture and population genetics of Yumin Culture in North China steppe ecotone, Science Advances
Ye Canyang et al.(2023) Adze-shaped lithic production of Yumin Culture across Hebei-Inner Mongolia border, Acta Anthropologica Sinica

name: Hongshan Culture
desc: Late Neolithic culture of the West Liao River basin in southeastern Inner Mongolia, western Liaoning and northern Hebei (4700–2900 BCE). Famous for sophisticated jade artifacts (pig-dragons, coiled dragons, cloud-shaped pendants), the Niuheliang ritual complex featuring the Goddess Temple, stone cairn tombs and altar platforms — one of the earliest complex societies in East Asia with state-level ritual organization. Economy based on millet farming, pig husbandry, hunting and fishing; painted pottery with geometric and zoomorphic designs. Ancient genomes indicate predominant Ancient Northeast Asian (ANA) ancestry with early millet farmer admixture from the Yellow River basin. Core ancestral substrate for later Bronze Age cultures of Northeast China and the Mongolian steppe interface.

sites.name: Niuheliang
sites.desc: Supreme Hongshan ritual centre in Chaoyang (Liaoning); Goddess Temple with clay female figurines, stone cairn tombs, pyramidal altar platform, and jade workshop — the earliest known state-level ceremonial complex in East Asia, dated 3800–2900 BCE

sites.name: Hongshanhou
sites.desc: Type site near Chifeng (Inner Mongolia); eponymous settlement mound defining the Hongshan Culture, first excavated in 1908, two-phase stratigraphy with painted pottery and incised clay

sites.name: Weijiawopu
sites.desc: Large Hongshan settlement west of Chifeng; clustered semi-subterranean houses, abundant pottery and jade-working debris, dated 4400–3600 BCE

sites.name: Dongshanzui
sites.desc: Ritual platform site near Niuheliang; stone altar with clay pregnant-female figurines, earliest evidence of fertility worship in Northeast China

sites.name: Sanguan Dianzi
sites.desc: Late Hongshan settlement cluster in Kazuo County; stone-foundation houses, jade bi discs and slit rings marking social hierarchy

papers.title: Guo et al. (2020) The Hongshan Culture and the origins of Chinese jade carving, Antiquity
Ning et al. (2020) Ancient genomes from the Hongshan Culture and the formation of ancestral populations in Northeast China, Current Biology
Peterson & Shelach (2012) The Hongshan Culture and the emergence of complex societies in Northeast China, Journal of World Prehistory

name: Taosi Culture
desc: Late Neolithic walled-town culture of the Linfen Basin, southern Shanxi (2300–1900 BCE), considered the most politically complex polity of the Longshan period on the Central Plains. The Taosi settlement — the largest known walled site in prehistoric China at 280 ha — contained a rammed-clay enclosure, palatial compounds, elite burials with painted wooden coffins, jade and copper bells, and an astronomical observatory platform with pillar slots for solar solstice alignment, the oldest such structure in East Asia. Millet-based agriculture with pig and sheep husbandry; written symbols on pottery vessels possibly ancestral to Chinese script. Widely linked to the legendary Xia dynasty and the capital of Emperor Yao recorded in classical texts.

sites.name: Taosi
sites.desc: Type site in Xiangfen County (Shanxi); 280 ha walled settlement with rammed-earth enclosure, palace foundations, elite cemetery with jade and copper artefacts, and the oldest astronomical observatory in East Asia — a semi-circular pillar-slot platform for sunrise observation

sites.name: Zhoujiazhuang
sites.desc: Large Taosi residential area north of the main enclosure; clustered pit-houses, storage cellars and pottery kilns, dated to Middle Taosi period

sites.name: Nanwa
sites.desc: Peripheral Taosi settlement cluster on the Fen River floodplain; millet-grinding tools and ritual vessels, lower-status artisans' quarter

sites.name: Fangcheng
sites.desc: Southern satellite walled site in Yicheng County; small elite centre with rammed-earth fortifications contemporary with Taosi

sites.name: Gaosi
sites.desc: Western Taosi cemetery complex; ranked burials with painted lacquer-wood coffins and jade yue axes, documenting social stratification

papers.title: Zehui Chen (2025, Chinese Academy of Sciences) Ancient DNA from Shimao city records kinship practices in Neolithic China
He Nu (2013) The Taosi site and the formation of early states in the Central Plains, Chinese Archaeology
Pankenier et al. (2008) The Xiangfen Taosi site: a Chinese Neolithic observatory, Archaeologia Baltica
Gao et al. (2020) Ancient DNA evidence from Taosi reveals genetic history of the pre-Xia Central Plains, Science Bulletin

name: Shimao Culture
desc: Late Neolithic to early Bronze Age stone-walled city culture of the northern Loess Plateau, Shenmu County, Shaanxi (2300–1800 BCE). The Shimao site — at 400 ha the largest known walled site of its period in China — features inner and outer stone fortifications with gates, turrets and watchtowers, a 70 m stepped pyramidal palace platform (Huang Cheng Tai), jade-inlaid city walls, and approximately 80 sacrificial skulls beneath the southern city gate. Advanced jade carving, copper metallurgy, mural painting and stone relief sculpture; millet and livestock economy at the agro-pastoral ecotone of the Ordos frontier. Genetic data reveal mixed Yellow River farmer and steppe-related ancestry, reflecting its strategic position at the interface of early Chinese civilization and Eurasian steppe cultures.

sites.name: Shimao
sites.desc: Type site in Shenmu County (Shaanxi); the largest known 3rd millennium BC walled city in China — 400 ha inner and outer stone fortifications, 11-tier stepped pyramid palace platform reaching 70 m, jade ritual objects embedded in walls, mass sacrificial burials of young females beneath the southern gate

sites.name: Xinhua
sites.desc: Shimao elite cemetery on a hill south of the main city; 30+ stone-cist burials with jade cong tubes, bi discs, yue axes and bronze arrowheads, documenting ranked social hierarchy

sites.name: Nianzuitou
sites.desc: Northern Shimao settlement cluster; stone-foundation houses and pastoral activity areas, transitional agro-pastoral subsistence at the Ordos steppe margin

sites.name: Zhihong
sites.desc: Shimao-period cemetery and ritual zone on the Tuwei River east bank; jade plaques and painted pottery hinting at interregional exchange with the Central Plains

sites.name: Xishan
sites.desc: Shimao outer satellite settlement in Gaojiabao; stone-walled residential compound with multiple semi-subterranean houses and livestock enclosures

papers.title: Sun et al. (2016) The discovery and excavation of the Shimao city site, Shaanxi, Chinese Archaeology
Jaang et al. (2018) Shimao and the rise of complex societies on the Loess Plateau, Antiquity
Yang et al. (2021) Ancient DNA reveals Shimao as a cosmopolitan centre at the steppe-farming interface, Nature Communications

name: Zhukaigou Culture
desc: Early Bronze Age culture of the Ordos Plateau in south-central Inner Mongolia (2000–1400 BCE), known from the type site at Zhukaigou in Ejin Horo Banner. Represents a transitional agro-pastoral society at the steppe-farming interface, initially reliant on millet agriculture and pig husbandry in earlier phases, shifting progressively toward sheep, goat and cattle pastoralism and greater mobility over time — a trajectory that mirrors the broader Bronze Age pastoralisation of the northern Chinese frontier. Distinctive ceramic assemblage includes li tripods, liyan steamers, guan jars and basins, predominantly hand-made with cord-marked, basket-impressed and polished surface finishes, continuing the local Longshanoid ceramic tradition. Bronze artefacts appear by the Middle Zhukaigou phase (c. 1700 BCE): small tools, knives, awls, earrings and arrowheads, reflecting early local bronze-working knowledge transmitted through the Hexi Corridor from the Qijia Culture and further west. Burials are predominantly shaft pit tombs with flexed supine inhumations, occasionally with stone or timber chamber linings. Genetic profiles from Zhukaigou remains indicate predominant Yellow River millet farmer ancestry (O2, N) with detectable steppe-related (Q1a, R1a) admixture, consistent with its position as a conduit between the Central Plains Bronze Age and the Eurasian steppe. The culture declines after 1400 BCE as increasing aridity and the expansion of pastoral nomads transform the Ordos steppe.

sites.name: Zhukaigou
sites.desc: Type site in Ejin Horo Banner (Ordos, Inner Mongolia); excavated 1977–1984, 83 shaft pit tombs and 10 house foundations spanning three phases (Early c.2000–1700, Middle c.1700–1500, Late c.1500–1400 BCE), documenting the gradual transition from settled agriculture to mobile pastoralism, with earliest local bronze tools appearing in Middle phase deposits

sites.name: Xinhai
sites.desc: Zhukaigou cemetery in Jungar Banner (Ordos); 30+ shaft pit tombs with cord-marked pottery, sheep-goat bones and bronze earrings, transitional agro-pastoral assemblage from the Middle Zhukaigou phase

sites.name: Zhaimao
sites.desc: Zhukaigou period settlement and cemetery in Fugu County (Shaanxi), on the south bank of the Yellow River opposite Ordos; stone-lined pit burials with ceramic li tripods and bronzes, marking the southern extent of Zhukaigou influence

sites.name: Nanshan
sites.desc: Zhukaigou settlement site in Dongsheng District (Ordos); clustered semi-subterranean houses, ash pits with carbonised millet, sheep and cattle bones, and stone harvesting knives, representing the agricultural base of early Zhukaigou economy

sites.name: Xishan
sites.desc: Late Zhukaigou cemetery in Jungar Banner (Ordos); predominance of sheep-goat remains in burials, reduced pig frequency and increased bronze ornament frequency, marking the late-phase pastoral shift c.1500–1400 BCE

papers.title: Shelach (2009) Prehistoric societies on the northern frontiers of China: archaeological perspectives on identity formation and change during the first millennium BCE, Equinox
Li (2018) The Zhukaigou Culture and the formation of the northern Chinese Bronze Age frontier, Antiquity
Ning et al. (2020) Ancient genomes from northern China suggest links between subsistence changes and human migration, Nature Communications

name: Qijia Culture
desc: Early Bronze Age culture of the upper Yellow River region in Gansu and eastern Qinghai (2300–1500 BCE), known for the earliest extensive copper and bronze metallurgy in China. Centred in the Tao, Daxia and Yellow River valleys, Qijia communities practised millet and wheat agriculture with sheep, cattle and pig husbandry. Distinctive ceramic assemblage includes high-shouldered jars with double loop handles, painted and cord-marked vessels. Elite burials contain bronze knives, axes, mirrors (the earliest known in China), jade ornaments, and sacrificed animals. Positioned at the crossroads of the Eurasian steppe Hexi Corridor and the Central Plains, Qijia transmitted bronze-working technology, domesticated wheat and barley, and horse-related knowledge eastward while funnelling Chinese jade and painted pottery westward — a key node in early East-West interaction networks. Genetic profiles show dominant Yellow River farmer ancestry with minor steppe-related admixture.

sites.name: Qijiaping
sites.desc: Type site on the Tao River, Guanghe County (Gansu); eponymous settlement and cemetery complex — 55 ha with house foundations, pottery kilns and 118 burials, including the earliest bronze mirror in China, excavated 1924 by Andersson and 1975 by the Gansu Provincial Institute

sites.name: Mogou
sites.desc: Large Qijia cemetery in Lintan County (Gansu); over 1,500 shaft-and-chamber tombs with abundant burial goods — bronze knives, bone arrowheads, jade beads and painted pottery, providing the largest Qijia mortuary dataset for social stratification analysis

sites.name: Lajia
sites.desc: Catastrophically buried Qijia settlement in Minhe County (Qinghai); destroyed c.1900 BCE by earthquake-triggered mudflow — preserved in situ human skeletons, house interiors and daily utensils, the 'Pompeii of China' — key for understanding early Bronze Age domestic life in the Yellow River basin

sites.name: Huangniangniangtai
sites.desc: Western Qijia outpost in Wuwei (Hexi Corridor); copper-smelting furnace remains and slag, bronze knife moulds and finished tools, confirming local metallurgical production and controlling east-west exchange along the Gansu Corridor

sites.name: Dahezhuang
sites.desc: Large Qijia settlement in Yongjing County (Gansu); clustered pit-house compound with storage pits, carbonised millet and wheat grains, sheep and cattle bones, documenting the mixed farming-pastoral economy at the core of Qijia territory

papers.title: Chen et al. (2015) Qijia culture and the earliest copper metallurgy in China, Journal of Archaeological Science
papers.org: University of Science and Technology of China

papers.title: Womack et al. (2017) Mapping Qijiaping: new work on the type-site of the Qijia culture, Journal of Field Archaeology
Wang et al. (2021) Ancient DNA reveals the genetic history of the Qijia culture and early Bronze Age populations of the Yellow River region, Molecular Biology and Evolution

name: Xia Dynasty
desc: First Chinese dynasty (2070–1600 BCE) in traditional historiography, widely equated with the Erlitou culture of the Yiluo River valley in western Henan. The Erlitou site (c.1900–1500 BCE) was a planned urban centre of 300+ ha with a palatial compound, rammed-earth foundations, bronze workshops producing ritual vessels (jue, jia, ding) and weapons, elite burials with turquoise-inlaid bronze plaques and cowrie shells, and the earliest known bronze foundry in East Asia. Millet and rice agriculture with pig, sheep and cattle husbandry; advanced ceramic industry with distinctive dark grey burnished wares. The Xia dynasty is recorded in Sima Qian's Shiji and the Bamboo Annals as the founding dynasty of Chinese civilization, credited with controlling the Yellow River flood and establishing hereditary succession. The Erlitou polity abruptly declined c.1500 BCE, coinciding with the rise of the Shang. Ancient genomes from Erlitou show dominant Yellow River farmer ancestry (O2, N) with limited peripheral admixture.

sites.name: Erlitou
sites.desc: Type site and capital of the Xia dynasty / Erlitou culture in Yanshi (Luoyang, Henan); 300+ ha urban centre with a palace complex of rammed-earth foundations, elite bronze foundry producing the earliest ritual bronze vessels in China (jue, jia, ding), and turquoise-inlaid bronze plaques — widely regarded as the archaeological correlate of the Xia dynasty capital

sites.name: Xinzhai
sites.desc: Fortified Xia-period settlement in Xinmi (Henan); double moat and rammed-earth enclosure, transitional Longshan-to-Erlitou cultural layers, key for understanding the formation of early Xia political power

sites.name: Dongxiafeng
sites.desc: Xia cultural site in Xia County (Shanxi); bronze-foundry remains and Erlitou-style ritual vessels, marking the westward expansion of Erlitou/Xia influence into the Yuncheng Basin

sites.name: Huizui
sites.desc: Erlitou-period settlement and stone-tool workshop in Yanshi (Henan); specialised lithic production centre supplying the Erlitou elite with limestone and slate implements

sites.name: Nanshui
sites.desc: Xia-period cemetery cluster on the Yi River (Henan); ranked burials with graded grave goods documenting the emerging social hierarchy of Erlitou/Xia society

papers.title: Liu (2004) The Chinese Neolithic: Trajectories to Early States, Cambridge University Press
Xu (2012) The Erlitou Culture and the Xia Dynasty: a review of the evidence, Journal of East Asian Archaeology
Ning et al. (2020) Ancient genomes from the Hongshan Culture and the formation of ancestral populations in Northeast China, Current Biology

name: Shang Dynasty
desc: The earliest archaeologically confirmed Chinese dynasty (1600–1046 BCE), centred on the Yellow River floodplain in northern Henan and western Shandong. The Shang state emerged after the overthrow of the Xia and built large rammed-earth walled cities at Zhengzhou (c.1600–1400 BCE, 25 km²) and Yanshi (c.1550–1300 BCE), followed by the final capital at Yinxu near Anyang (c.1300–1046 BCE). Famous for the earliest known Chinese writing — oracle bone script incised on turtle plastrons and cattle scapulae for royal divination — recording kings, rituals, warfare, agriculture and astronomy. Advanced bronze metallurgy produced massive ritual vessels (ding, gui, zun, you) with detailed taotie animal-face motifs, cast using piece-mould technology unique to China. Elite burials at Yinxu include the undisturbed Tomb of Fu Hao (c.1200 BCE) with over 1,600 artefacts — bronze vessels, jade carvings, bone hairpins, ivory and 16 human sacrificial victims. Mass human and animal sacrifice was practised in royal burials and foundation rituals. The Shang was overthrown by the Zhou at the Battle of Muye c.1046 BCE. Genetic profiles from Shang-era sites show predominantly Yellow River farmer ancestry (O2, N) with minor admixture from northern populations.

sites.name: Yinxu
sites.desc: Type site and final Shang capital near Anyang (Henan); excavated from 1928 — palatial foundations, royal cemeteries with 13+ large ramped tombs, over 150,000 oracle bone fragments with the earliest Chinese script, and the Tomb of Fu Hao (queen of King Wu Ding) with 1,600+ exquisite bronze, jade and bone artefacts

sites.name: Zhengzhou Shang City
sites.desc: Early Shang capital (c.1600–1400 BCE) in Zhengzhou (Henan); a 7 km rammed-earth enclosure enclosing 25 km² — the largest walled site of its time in East Asia — with bronze foundries, bone workshops, and elite burials containing early Shang bronze ritual vessels

sites.name: Yanshi Shang City
sites.desc: Early Shang walled capital near the Erlitou site in Yanshi (Henan); rammed-earth inner and outer walls, palatial compound, bronze and pottery workshops, built after the conquest of the Xia as a strategic administrative centre

sites.name: Huanbei
sites.desc: Middle Shang walled city (c.1450–1250 BCE) on the Huan River north of Yinxu; a 4.7 km rammed-earth perimeter, palace foundations, and bronze casting kilns — the immediate precursor to the late Shang capital at Yinxu

sites.name: Subutun
sites.desc: Large Shang elite cemetery in Qingzhou (Shandong); monumental 'four-ram' bronze zun vessel and chariot burials documenting the Shang presence in the eastern territories beyond the core Henan region

papers.title: Keightley (2000) The Ancestral Landscape: Time, Space, and Community in Late Shang China, University of California Press
Bagley (1999) Shang Archaeology, in The Cambridge History of Ancient China, Cambridge University Press
Campbell (2018) The Archaeology of the Shang Dynasty, Journal of Archaeological Research

name: Zhou Dynasty
desc: The longest-lasting Chinese dynasty (1046–256 BCE), founded after King Wu of Zhou overthrew the Shang at the Battle of Muye c.1046 BCE. The Western Zhou period (1046–771 BCE) was centred on the Wei River valley in Shaanxi (capital at Haojing, near modern Xi'an), with a second capital at Chengzhou (Luoyang) controlling the eastern territories. The Zhou introduced the Mandate of Heaven doctrine to justify dynastic rule, and operated a feudal-like system of regional states. The Eastern Zhou period (770–256 BCE) saw the capital moved to Luoyang, the decline of royal authority, and the rise of competing states during the Spring and Autumn and Warring States periods. Western Zhou is famous for its ritual bronze vessels with lengthy cast inscriptions documenting land grants, military campaigns and royal decrees — forming the earliest extended Chinese texts beyond oracle bones. Iron-working appeared by the 8th c. BCE, centuries before Europe. The Zhou produced classic texts including the I Ching, Shijing, Shangshu and the Zuo Zhuan. Zhou bronzes, jade work and burials reached high sophistication. The dynasty ended in 256 BCE when the Zhou king surrendered to the state of Qin. Genetic profiles from Zhou-era sites show Yellow River farmer ancestry (O2, N) with increasing admixture from northern steppe populations as the Zhou interacted with the northern frontier.

sites.name: Haojing (Fenghao)
sites.desc: Western Zhou capital near Xi'an (Shaanxi); paired settlements of Hao and Feng on opposite banks of the Feng River — palace and temple foundations, bronze foundry, elite burials with inscribed bronze vessels documenting Zhou kings and nobles, the political centre of the Zhou realm 1046–771 BCE

sites.name: Zhouyuan
sites.desc: Pre-dynastic Zhou ancestral centre and early Western Zhou ritual site in Fufeng (Shaanxi); the Zhouyuan oracle bone cache and the famed 'He zun' vessel bearing the earliest known character for 'China' (Zhai / Zhongguo), excavated from a Zhou elite hoard

sites.name: Liyu
sites.desc: Western Zhou cemetery cluster in Sanmenxia (Henan); large elite tomb of the Guo state ruler with bronze ding vessels, chariot pit with 36 horses, jade and gold artefacts — and the legendary 'Sword of Goujian' found in a later context

sites.name: Tianma-Qucun
sites.desc: Site of the Jin state capital (Quwo, Shanxi) during Western Zhou; 19 large tombs of Jin marquises with ranked bronze vessel sets, the most complete Western Zhou feudal state cemetery discovered to date, confirming Zhou ritual protocols for regional lords

sites.name: Luoyang (Chengzhou)
sites.desc: Eastern Zhou capital (770–256 BCE) at Luoyang (Henan); the Wangcheng walled enclosure, large bronze foundries and workshops, elite tombs with Eastern Zhou bronze ritual vessels, jade and early iron implements — seat of Zhou royal authority during the Spring and Autumn and Warring States periods

papers.title: Shaughnessy (1991) Sources of Western Zhou History: Inscribed Bronze Vessels, University of California Press
Hsu & Linduff (1988) Western Zhou Civilization, Yale University Press
Li (2006) The Formation of the Chinese Civilization: An Archaeological Perspective, Yale University Press

name: Han Dynasty
desc: The second imperial dynasty of China (206 BCE–220 CE), succeeding the Qin and ruling after a brief interregnum by the Xin dynasty (9–23 CE). The Western Han (206 BCE–9 CE) was based at Chang'an (Xi'an) in the Wei River valley; the Eastern Han (25–220 CE) moved its capital east to Luoyang. At its peak under Emperor Wu (r.141–87 BCE), the Han empire extended from the Korean Peninsula in the east to the Tarim Basin in the west, from the Gobi Desert in the north to northern Vietnam in the south — roughly 6 million km², one of the largest polities of the ancient world. The Han period saw the formal establishment of Confucianism as state ideology, the introduction of the imperial civil service examination system, the opening of the Silk Road, the invention of paper, major advances in astronomy, medicine and iron and bronze metallurgy, and the composition of Sima Qian's Records of the Grand Historian. Han material culture is defined by elaborate ceramic and bronze vessels, jade burial suits sewn with gold wire, monumental stone tomb carvings, glazed pottery tomb figurines (mingqi) depicting cavalry, infantry, farmsteads and entertainers, and vast quantities of Wuzhu bronze coinage. The Xiongnu confederation was the Han's principal rival; decades of war, tribute and marriage alliances culminated in the Han defeat and fragmentation of the northern Xiongnu in the 1st–2nd c. CE, opening the Gansu Corridor and Tarim Basin to Chinese control and Silk Road traffic. Genetic profiles from Han-era sites in the Central Plains show dominant Yellow River farmer ancestry (O2, N) with minor steppe admixture, while frontier garrisons in the northwest carried significantly more northern Eurasian ancestry.

sites.name: Chang'an (Xi'an)
sites.desc: Western Han capital (206 BCE–9 CE), one of the largest cities of the ancient world (~36 km², 250,000+ inhabitants); rammed-earth city wall with 12 gates, palatial complex of Weiyang Palace covering 5 km², armouries, granaries, markets and elite residential wards; starting point of the Silk Road

sites.name: Mawangdui
sites.desc: Extraordinary Western Han tomb complex near Changsha (Hunan); 2nd c. BCE burials of Marquis Dai and family — the perfectly preserved body of Lady Dai (Xin Zhui), over 1,000 lacquerware, silk textiles, manuscripts on silk (I Ching, Dao De Jing, divination texts, star maps), and a painted silk funerary banner — the richest single Han burial ever excavated

sites.name: Yangling
sites.desc: Mausoleum of Emperor Jing (r.157–141 BCE) near Xi'an (Shaanxi); 84 burial pits surrounding the tomb mound containing over 50,000 ceramic figurines — infantry, cavalry, servants, acrobats and domestic animals — plus iron weapons, chariots and inscribed tiles, providing an unparalleled snapshot of Western Han military and court life

sites.name: Luoyang (Eastern Han)
sites.desc: Eastern Han capital (25–190 CE); twin walled city covering 10 km² with palatial compound, Mingtang ritual hall, and the White Horse Temple (Baimasi) — the first Buddhist temple in China, founded under Emperor Ming in 68 CE, marking the arrival of Buddhism via the Silk Road

sites.name: Kongquehe (Tarim Basin)
sites.desc: Han frontier garrison and watchtower complex in the Lop Desert (Xinjiang); series of beacon towers, barracks and granaries along the Silk Road, with Han bamboo slips, silk fragments, Wuzhu coins and weapons — the westernmost direct Han military presence

papers.title: Loewe (1986) The Cambridge History of China, Volume I: the Ch'in and Han Empires, 221 BC – AD 220, Cambridge University Press
Hansen (2012) The Silk Road: A New History, Oxford University Press
Kenderdine et al. (2020) Ancient DNA and the genetic history of the Han dynasty, Archaeological and Anthropological Sciences

name: Scythian Culture
desc: Iron Age nomadic culture of the Pontic-Caspian steppe (700–200 BCE), famous from Herodotus' Histories and spectacular kurgan burials laden with gold. Highly mobile horse-riding pastoral economy with seasonal migrations; sophisticated animal-style goldwork — plaques, pectorals, scabbard covers, vessel fittings — blending Greek, Persian and local traditions. Royal kurgans of the 4th c. BCE reached 15+ m in height and contained multiple chambers, sacrificed horses and retainers, and tons of imported Greek wine amphorae. Military elite using composite bows, akinakes daggers, and scale armour. Scythian material culture extended from the Danube delta east to the Don River, north to the forest-steppe boundary. Genetic profiles show a mix of indigenous Srubnaya-derived steppe ancestry and influx from eastern steppe populations during the Iron Age.

sites.name: Solokha
sites.desc: Royal Scythian kurgan on the lower Dnieper (Zaporizhzhia, Ukraine); 4th c. BCE, two chambers — one unlooted — yielding the famous gold pectoral, a ceremonial akinakes with gold scabbard, and silver amphorae, masterpieces of Greco-Scythian toreutics

sites.name: Chertomlyk
sites.desc: Massive 4th-c. BCE royal kurgan near Nikopol (Ukraine); 20 m high, 350 m circumference, with four burial chambers, 11 sacrificed horses, and a gold-and-silver gorytos sheath featuring scenes from the Achilles myth

sites.name: Aleksandropol
sites.desc: The largest Scythian kurgan in the Dnieper region (4th c. BCE); 21 m tall, containing multiple elite burials and thousands of gold appliqués, excavated in the 1850s and re-examined 2004–2009

sites.name: Melgunov Kurgan
sites.desc: Oldest known Scythian royal kurgan (7th c. BCE) near Kropyvnytskyi (Ukraine); earliest Scythian grave goods in the Pontic steppe, gold eagle plaques and Near Eastern-style artefacts marking the initial Scythian intrusion from the east

sites.name: Kamenskoe Gorodishche
sites.desc: Large fortified Scythian settlement on the lower Dnieper; bronze-working quarter, imported Greek pottery and amphorae, the major industrial and trade centre of 4th–3rd c. BCE Scythia

papers.title: Rolle (1989) The World of the Scythians, University of California Press
Murphy et al. (2023) Ancient genomes trace the origin and decline of the Scythians, Science Advances
Parzinger (2021) The Scythians: Nomad Warriors of the Steppe, Antiquity

name: Saka Culture
desc: Iron Age eastern nomadic culture of the Kazakh steppe, Semirechye, Tian Shan foothills and Altai (900–200 BCE), known in Persian inscriptions as the Sakā and forming the eastern branch of the broader Scytho-Siberian world. Horse-mounted pastoralists with composite-bow archery and Animal Style art; monumental royal kurgans with timber burial chambers, gold-foil ornamentation on clothing and weaponry (Issyk 'Golden Man'), and large-scale horse sacrifices (Berel, Arzhan). Key node in east-west exchange transmitting Achaemenid silver, Chinese silk and mirrors, and steppe gold across Eurasia. Genetic ancestry dominated by steppe pastoralist (Andronovo-related) sources with increasing East Asian admixture towards the eastern Saka range.

sites.name: Issyk
sites.desc: Royal Saka kurgan in Semirechye (50 km east of Almaty, Kazakhstan); 6 m high, 60 m diameter, 4th–3rd c. BCE — excavated 1969 by Kemal Akishev, containing the 'Golden Man' warrior with 4,000+ gold ornaments, a silver cup bearing the Issyk inscription (possibly the earliest Saka script), and horse-gear

sites.name: Arzhan
sites.desc: Earliest known Saka royal kurgan in Tuva (9th–8th c. BCE); Arzhan-1 and Arzhan-2 — the latter containing over 5,700 gold items (panther plaques, belt buckles, torcs), ranking among the richest Scythian-era tombs ever found

sites.name: Berel
sites.desc: Elite Saka burial ground in the Altai Mountains (East Kazakhstan); permafrost-preserved timber chambers, tattooed human remains, and 13+ sacrificed horses in full tack, 4th–3rd c. BCE

sites.name: Shilikty
sites.desc: Saka royal necropolis in East Kazakhstan (8th–5th c. BCE); massive kurgans up to 100 m diameter, gold animal-style jewellery, a gold 'sun-crown' diadem, and bronze cauldrons — the easternmost major Saka elite centre

sites.name: Tasmola
sites.desc: Type site of the Tasmola culture variant in central Kazakhstan (7th–5th c. BCE); distinctive kurgans with 'moustache' stone alignments, bronze arrowheads, and Saka animal-style horse gear

papers.title: Akishev (1978) The Golden Man of Issyk and the Saka culture of Semirechye, Kazakh Academy of Sciences
Chang et al. (2017) The Saka and the beginnings of nomadic pastoralism in the Kazakh steppe, Archaeological Research in Asia
Unterländer et al. (2017) Ancestry and demography of the Saka of the Eurasian steppe, Nature Communications

name: Slab‑Grave Culture
desc: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age culture of Mongolia, Transbaikalia and northern China (1100–400 BCE), defined by rectangular stone-slab-lined burial cists covered with stone mounds. Successor to the Deer Stone-Khirigsuur Complex in eastern and central Mongolia, expanding north to Lake Baikal. Pastoral herders with mixed sheep, goat, cattle and horse economy; material culture includes bronze arrowheads, pipe-shaped and curved animal-style plaques, shell and stone beads, bone implements and coarse handmade pottery. Genetic profiles show predominantly Ancient Northeast Asian (ANA) ancestry derived from Ulaanzuukh-Tevsh and DSKC populations, with continuity into the later Xiongnu period. Slab graves are the most numerous Bronze/Early Iron Age monument type in Mongolia, with thousands of known examples across the eastern and central steppe.

sites.name: Maikhan Tolgoi
sites.desc: Key slab-grave complex in the Upper Orkhon Valley (central Mongolia); 60+ slab burial cists with abundant radiocarbon dates (1100–400 BCE) and ancient genome samples, the type reference for Slab-Grave funerary architecture in central Mongolia

sites.name: Burkhan Buudai
sites.desc: Large slab-grave necropolis in Töv Province (central Mongolia); rectangular stone cists oriented E–W, bronze arrowhead deposits and animal scapula offerings

sites.name: Ulaan Zuukh
sites.desc: Transitional cemetery in Dornod (eastern Mongolia) with slab graves overlapping earlier Ulaanzuukh-Tevesh figure-shaped burials, documenting the cultural evolution from LBA to EIA in eastern Mongolia

sites.name: Khyaraatyn Gol
sites.desc: Northern slab-grave cluster in Buryatia (Transbaikalia, Russia); stone-cist burials with bronze harness fittings and shell beads, marking the northern expansion of the Slab-Grave culture to the Selenga River basin

sites.name: Dornod Steppe Cluster
sites.desc: Extensive scatter of slab graves across the eastern Mongolian steppe; hundreds of E–W aligned rectangular cists, surface bronze arrowhead finds and occasional deer-stone re-use, documenting the dense settlement of east Mongolia during the EIA

papers.title: Tsybiktarov (1998) Slab Grave Culture of Transbaikalia and Mongolia, Ulan-Ude (comprehensive monograph on 542 tombs)
Miyamoto et al. (2016) Radiocarbon dating of slab graves in Mongolia, Radiocarbon
Lee et al. (2025) Ancient genomes from the Slab Grave culture reveal the formation of Early Iron Age Mongolia, Nature Communications

name: Karasuk Culture
desc: Late Bronze Age culture of the Minusinsk Basin, Khakassia and the Altai-Sayan region (1500–800 BCE), known for its distinctive bronze daggers with curved animal-head hilts, socketed arrowheads, and tin-bronze metallurgy. Pastoral economy with cattle, sheep and horse herding; settled in permanent villages with timber-framed pit-houses. Famous for the Karasuk bronze casting tradition — curved knives, socketed celts and personal ornaments featuring the 'curl' and 'eye' motifs — and the easternmost distribution of Seima-Turbino type metalwork. Genetic profiles show a mix of Andronovo-derived steppe ancestry and local Siberian (Okunev-related) populations, with emerging East Asian admixture. Considered a key ancestral substrate for the subsequent Tagar Culture of the Iron Age Minusinsk Basin.

sites.name: Karasuk
sites.desc: Type site on the Karasuk River near Bateni, Minusinsk Basin; eponymous cemetery with classic bronze daggers and socketed arrowheads, defining the Karasuk material culture

sites.name: Bystrovka
sites.desc: Major Karasuk settlement with multiple pit-houses, bronze casting workshop debris and imported Central Asian ornaments

sites.name: Yarki
sites.desc: Rich Karasuk kurgan cemetery in the Upper Yenisei valley, yielding tin-bronze knives, stone scepters and early iron objects

sites.name: Malinovka
sites.desc: Large settlement complex with Karasuk and late Andronovo cultural layers; key for radiocarbon chronology of the transition

sites.name: Sukhanikha
sites.desc: Well-preserved Karasuk burial ground with intact grave assemblages, classic animal-head bronze daggers and pottery

papers.title: Legrand (2006) The Karasuk culture and the evolution of bronze metallurgy in the Minusinsk Basin, Archaeology, Ethnology and Anthropology of Eurasia
Childebayeva et al. (2024) Bronze Age Northern Eurasian genetics in the context of development of metallurgy and Siberian ancestry
papers.org: Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology

name: Sarmatian Culture
desc: Iron Age nomadic confederation of the Pontic-Caspian steppe (600 BCE–400 CE), expanding from the Don–Volga region westward into the Pontic steppe, north Caucasus and eastern Europe. Horse-riding pastoral economy with seasonally mobile herding; famous for richly furnished elite burials with gold, silver and bronze ornaments, iron long swords (spatha), composite bows, lamellar armour and cloisonné-decorated metalwork. Sarmatian women are archaeologically attested as armed warriors — likely the source of Greek Amazon traditions. Successors to the Scythians in the Pontic steppe, eventually forming a Sarmatian-dominated zone from the Danube to the Urals. Genetic profiles show a mix of Srubnaya-derived steppe ancestry with increasing Central Asian and East Asian admixture, mediated by contact with Sauromatian and Saka populations.

sites.name: Kobyakovo
sites.desc: Major Sarmatian fortified settlement and burial ground at the mouth of the Don River (Rostov, Russia); rich 1st–2nd c. CE graves with gold torcs, Roman imports and cloisonné eagle brooches, key site for Sarmatian chronology

sites.name: Khokhlach
sites.desc: Elite Sarmatian kurgan near Novocherkassk (1st c. CE); famous for the 'Novocherkassk Treasure' — gold bracelets, torque, diadem with inlay, silver vessels, and ceremonial iron swords with gold scabbard fittings

sites.name: Poroghi
sites.desc: Sarmatian kurgan group in Moldova (2nd–1st c. BCE); female warrior burial with iron sword, arrowheads, bronze cauldron and Greek black-glaze pottery, supporting the warrior-woman archaeological profile

sites.name: Filipovka
sites.desc: Elite Sauromato-Sarmatian kurgan necropolis in the southern Urals (4th–3rd c. BCE); massive 80 m diameter kurgans with multiple chambers, deer-antler ceremonial headdresses overlaid with gold foil, gold vessel hoards, and hundreds of arrowheads

sites.name: Sokolova Mogila
sites.desc: Sarmatian royal kurgan on the lower Dnieper (1st c. CE); spectacular female elite burial with gold crown, Roman glass, bronze incense burners and a ceremonial silver rhyton, demonstrating Sarmatian control of Greek–Roman trade

papers.title: Davis-Kimball (1997) Warrior Women of the Eurasian Steppe, Archaeology
Abramova (2017) Sarmatians of the North Caucasus: Chronology and Cultural Links, Russian Academy of Sciences
Lazaridis et al. (2024) The genetic origin of the Indo-Europeans, bioRxiv

name: Timber-Grave Culture
desc: Late Bronze Age culture of the Pontic-Caspian steppe and forest-steppe (1900–1200 BCE), also known as Srubnaya culture from Russian сруб ('timber frame') referring to its characteristic timber-lined burial chambers. Pastoral agricultural economy with cattle, sheep, horse and some pig husbandry; settled and semi-settled communities in river valleys with wattle-and-daub houses. Bronze metallurgy producing socketed axes, spears, daggers and sickles; bone arrowheads and cheek-pieces for chariot/horse control. Srubnaya pottery is flat-based with geometric incised and cord-impressed decoration. Represents a westward extension of the Andronovo-related Late Bronze Age steppe cultural horizon, considered ancestral to the Cimmerian and early Scythian populations. Genetic profiles are dominated by R1a-Z93 steppe ancestry.

sites.name: Srubnaya
sites.desc: Type site of the Srubnaya culture in the Volga–Ural interfluve (Saratov region, Russia); timber-lined burial pits under kurgans defining the eponymous grave construction tradition

sites.name: Il'inka
sites.desc: Large Srubnaya settlement on the Seversky Donets River (Kharkiv region, Ukraine); multiple semi-subterranean houses with stone hearths, bronze casting workshops, and grain storage pits

sites.name: Berezhnovka
sites.desc: Srubnaya settlement complex on the Volga River (Volgograd region); excavated pit-houses with quern stones, bronze sickle fragments, and extensive faunal remains documenting mixed farming

sites.name: Pokrovka
sites.desc: Key Srubnaya burial ground in the Samara–Volga region; multiple kurgans with timber chambers, horse gear and bronze socketed axes, pivotal for Srubnaya–Andronovo interaction studies

papers.title: Otroshchenko (2003) The Srubnaya culture: chronology and periodisation, Eurasia Antiqua
papers.org: National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine

papers.title: Juras et al. (2024) Ancient DNA and the population history of the Pontic-Caspian steppe Bronze Age, Nature Communications

name: Abashevo Culture
desc: Middle Bronze Age culture of the Middle Volga and Kama River forest-steppe zone (2300–1800 BCE), known for rich kurgan burials with ochre, elaborate bronze weaponry (spears, socketed axes, knives), and the earliest eastward spread of Seima-Turbino type metalwork west of the Urals. Economy combined cattle and horse pastoralism with some agriculture in river valleys. Distinctive pottery with cord-impressed and comb-stamped geometric decoration. Abashevo is ancestral to the Sintashta culture and closely related to early Andronovo; its fortified settlements and metallurgical sophistication represent a key formative stage for the subsequent chariot-using Sintashta-Petrovka horizon. Genetic profiles are dominated by R1a-Z93 steppe ancestry with limited European farmer admixture.

sites.name: Abashevo
sites.desc: Type cemetery in Chuvashia (Middle Volga); eponymous burial ground with rich ochre-sprinkled inhumations, bronze socketed axes and spears, defining the Abashevo material culture

sites.name: Pepkino
sites.desc: Abashevo kurgan group on the Sura River (Chuvashia); large elite mound with multiple chambers, copper-bronze tools, and abundant cord-impressed pottery

sites.name: Ufa II
sites.desc: Multi-layer settlement at the confluence of the Ufa and Belaya Rivers; Abashevo through Srubnaya occupation layers documenting continuous Bronze Age habitation in the Ural foothills

sites.name: Balanbash
sites.desc: Abashevo kurgan and settlement complex in Tatarstan; key site linking Abashevo metalwork with the Seima-Turbino bronze casting tradition

papers.title: Chernykh (1992) Ancient Metallurgy in the USSR: The Early Metal Age, Cambridge University Press
Anthony (2007) The Horse, the Wheel, and Language

name: Lower Xiajiadian Culture
desc: Early Bronze Age culture of the West Liao River basin in southeastern Inner Mongolia and western Liaoning (2200–1600 BCE), succeeding the Hongshan Culture. Characterized by stone-fortified hilltop settlements with stone-paved foundations, defensive walls and bastions — among the earliest fortifications in East Asia. Economy based on millet agriculture, pig and sheep husbandry; sophisticated bronze metallurgy producing knives, rings, earrings and small tools, though large ritual vessels remained absent. Painted pottery with spiral and cloud-pattern designs continuing Hongshan artistic traditions. Jade working persisted but diminished in scale. Lower Xiajiadian is ancestral to the Upper Xiajiadian culture and represents the initial Bronze Age transition in Northeast China. Ancient genomes show predominantly Ancient Northeast Asian (ANA) ancestry with continued Yellow River millet farmer influence.

sites.name: Xiajiadian
sites.desc: Type site near Chifeng (Inner Mongolia); eponymous lower-layer settlement defining the Lower Xiajiadian culture, with stone house foundations, ash pits, and early bronze artefacts

sites.name: Dadianzi
sites.desc: Large Lower Xiajiadian cemetery and settlement in Aohan Banner (Inner Mongolia); over 800 burials excavated, rich grave goods including painted pottery, bronze ornaments, oracle bones and cowrie shells, providing a high-resolution cultural chronology

sites.name: Sanguan Dianzi
sites.desc: Fortified Lower Xiajiadian hilltop settlement in Kazuo County (Liaoning); double stone walls, gate towers, stone-paved house platforms, and bronze casting moulds

sites.name: Erdaojing
sites.desc: Lower Xiajiadian stone fortress complex on the upper Laoha River; interconnected walled enclosures with reservoir features, showing advanced defensive planning

papers.title: Shelach (1999) Leadership Strategies, Economic Activity, and Interregional Interaction: Social Complexity in Northeast China, Kluwer Academic/Plenum
Ning et al. (2020) Ancient genomes from the Hongshan Culture and the formation of ancestral populations in Northeast China, Current Biology

name: Tagar Culture
desc: Iron Age culture of the Minusinsk Basin, Khakassia and the upper Yenisei River (800–200 BCE), direct successor of the Karasuk Culture in southern Siberia. Famous for massive stone slab kurgans (up to 20 m diameter), richly furnished elite burials with bronze and iron daggers, battle-axes, bronze mirrors, belt plaques and massive bronze cauldrons in the Scytho-Siberian Animal Style. Economy based on semi-nomadic pastoralism — cattle, sheep, goats and horses — with seasonal movements between winter and summer pastures. Tagar metallurgists produced high-quality iron tools, socketed arrowheads (three-bladed trilobate type), and distinctive bronze 'Tagar cauldrons' with mushroom-shaped handles. Considered the Siberian Iron Age predecessor to the subsequent Tashtyk culture. Genetic profiles show continuity from Karasuk with additional influx of steppe pastoralist ancestry from the Saka world.

sites.name: Salbyk
sites.desc: The largest Tagar kurgan in the Minusinsk Basin ('Salbyk Palace'); 11 m high, 500 m circumference enclosure of standing stone slabs, weighing up to 50 tons each, 5th c. BCE — the monumental royal tomb of the Tagar elite

sites.name: Barsuchikha
sites.desc: Tagar kurgan group on the Yenisei River near Abakan; rich elite burials with bronze cauldrons, animal-style gold plaques, iron swords and Chinese-style bronzes documenting trans-Eurasian exchange

sites.name: Podgornoye
sites.desc: Type site of the Podgornoye variant phase of early Tagar (8th–6th c. BCE); timber and stone burial chambers, bronze arrowheads, and early iron knives

sites.name: Arzhan
sites.desc: Early nomadic royal kurgan in Tuva (9th–8th c. BCE), transitional Karasuk/Tagar period; Arzhan-1 and Arzhan-2 contain the richest Scythian-era gold assemblage in Siberia, with thousands of gold panther, deer and boar plaques

papers.title: Chlenova (1992) The Tagar Culture, in The Archaeology of the Steppes, BAR International Series
Murphy et al. (2023) Ancient genomes trace the origin and decline of the Scythians, Science Advances

name: Upper Xiajiadian Culture
desc: Late Bronze to Early Iron Age culture of the West Liao River basin in southeastern Inner Mongolia and western Liaoning (1200–600 BCE), succeeding the Lower Xiajiadian Culture. Known for distinctive bronze dagger-axes (ge), socketed arrowheads, horse bridles with antler cheek-pieces, and bronze animal-style plaques reflecting increasing interaction with the Eurasian steppe — including Scytho-Siberian motifs. Economy based on mixed pastoralism (sheep, goat, cattle, horse) and millet agriculture, with a shift toward greater mobility compared to the settled Lower Xiajiadian. Elite burials in stone-lined pits with bronze weapons, chariot gear and imported steppe bronzes. Upper Xiajiadian is considered the archaeological correlate of the proto-Donghu confederation, ancestral to later historical groups in eastern Mongolia and Manchuria. Genetic profiles show Ancient Northeast Asian (ANA) ancestry with notable influx of steppe pastoralist (R1a-Z93) and Siberian (Q1a) lineages.

sites.name: Xiajiadian Upper Layer
sites.desc: Type site; upper layer of the eponymous mound near Chifeng (Inner Mongolia), defining the Upper Xiajiadian culture with bronze daggers, horse gear, and stone-built tombs

sites.name: Dafu
sites.desc: Upper Xiajiadian cemetery in Fuxin County (Liaoning); over 200 burials with bronze dagger-axes, socketed arrowheads, and steppe-style animal plaques, a key assemblage for the Late Bronze Age transition in Northeast China

sites.name: Qianyang
sites.desc: Upper Xiajiadian settlement and bronze-casting workshop site near Chifeng; crucible fragments, stone moulds for dagger-axes and buttons, documenting local metallurgical production

sites.name: Zhongjiajie
sites.desc: Upper Xiajiadian stone fortress and burial complex in the Laohe River valley; well-preserved horse-gear assemblages with antler cheek-pieces and bronze bit fittings

papers.title: Shelach (1999) Leadership Strategies, Economic Activity, and Interregional Interaction: Social Complexity in Northeast China, Kluwer Academic/Plenum
Chao Ning (2020) Ancient genomes from northern China suggest links between subsistence changes and human migration

name: Xiongnu
desc: The first nomadic empire of the eastern Eurasian steppe (300 BCE–200 CE), centred on the Mongolian Plateau with its heartland in the Orkhon and Tuul River valleys. A multi-ethnic confederation of herder-warrior groups unified under a supreme ruler (chanyu), controlling a vast territory from Lake Baikal to the Ordos and from the Altai to Manchuria. Economy based on mobile pastoralism (horse, sheep, cattle, goat, camel) supplemented by tribute, trade and raids against Han Dynasty China; the Xiongnu extracted silk, grain, metals and luxury goods via the Heqin treaty system and controlled the eastern segments of the early Silk Road. Elite burials in massive timber-chambered kurgans under stone mounds at sites like Noin-Ula and Gol Mod, yielding Chinese lacquerware, silk, bronze mirrors, iron weapons, gold belt plaques with animal-style and geometric designs, and the earliest known steppe pile carpets. The Xiongnu also developed a runic-like script attested on bronze seals and wooden slips. Genetic profiles are highly heterogeneous: predominantly Northeast Asian (C2, N, Q1a) with significant western steppe (R1a, R1b) and southern (O2) admixture, reflecting the confederate nature of the empire. Xiongnu collapsed in the 2nd c. CE under internal fragmentation, Han military campaigns and the rise of the Xianbei.

sites.name: Noin-Ula
sites.desc: Royal Xiongnu cemetery in the Selenge River basin (Batsümber, northern Mongolia); 200+ kurgans, excavated 1924–1925 by Kozlov and again in the 2000s — permafrost-preserved organic artefacts including Chinese lacquerware (dated by inscription to 13 BCE), silk embroideries, a felt Hunnic carpet with griffin motifs, wooden chariot parts, and Han bronze mirrors

sites.name: Gol Mod
sites.desc: Xiongnu imperial cemetery in the Khogno Khan range (Bulgan, central Mongolia); massive elite kurgan with a timber burial chamber, gold belt plaques, silver horse trappings, Chinese bronze vessels, iron swords and a bronze seal of a Xiongnu official dated to the 1st c. BCE–1st c. CE

sites.name: Tsaraam
sites.desc: Xiongnu elite kurgan complex 20 km north of Noin-Ula (Tuul River valley); multi-chambered timber tombs with Chinese silk, lacquer ear cups, iron lamellar armour, gold foil ornaments and sacrificed horses, 1st c. BCE–1st c. CE

sites.name: Naimaa Tolgoi
sites.desc: Xiongnu kurgan cluster in the Gobi steppe (Ömnögovi, southern Mongolia); elite burials with silk, bronze mirrors, iron daggers, wooden combs, and horse gear

sites.name: Ivolga
sites.desc: Xiongnu fortified settlement and necropolis on the Selenge River (Buryatia, Russia); one of the few permanent Xiongnu settlements — defensive ramparts, pit-houses, iron smelting furnaces, imported Han pottery and coins

papers.title: Brosseder & Miller (2011) Xiongnu Archaeology: Multidisciplinary Perspectives of the First Steppe Empire in Inner Asia, Bonn Contributions to Asian Archaeology
Jeong et al. (2020) A dynamic 6,000-year genetic history of Eurasia's eastern steppe, Cell
Polosmak et al. (2008) The Noin-Ula Mounds: New Discoveries, Archaeology, Ethnology and Anthropology of Eurasia
